mnemonics.title: RSA Security Essentials
mnemonics.content: P-A-D-S: Primes must be strong, Add proper padding, Don't use small d (Wiener), Switch to 2048+ bits for security.

mnemonics.title: Remembering e = 65537
mnemonics.content: Sixteen Fives: 65537 = 2^16 + 1. Think '16 bits, prime exponent, two 1s (fast and secure!)'.

mnemonics.title: Key Size Recommendations
mnemonics.content: 2010: 1024 dead, 2048 ahead, 3072/4092 for what lies beyond.

mnemonics.title: Strong Prime Properties
mnemonics.content: P-1 and P+1 must have LARGE prime factors to resist Pollard's p-1 attacks.

acronyms.usage: Padding scheme that provides CCA security for RSA encryption
Attack model where attacker can decrypt any ciphertext of their choosing
Mathematical technique to speed up RSA decryption by computing modulo p and q separately
Most efficient classical algorithm for factoring large RSA moduli
Standards for RSA padding (PKCS#1 v1.5, OAEP, PSS)
Stronger CCA where attacker can request decryptions of ciphertexts adaptively

visualTips.visualization: Draw two boxes: Sender encrypts message m with public key (n,e) to get c = m^e mod n. Receiver decrypts with private key d: m = c^d mod n. Show that intercepting c doesn't help without d.
Think of n = p × q as a multiplication lock. Opening it requires finding the two prime factors. Like combining two large keys to make one lock - easy forward (multiply), hard backward (factor).
Show the number line: small d (left) = vulnerable, large d (right) = safe. Draw the continued fraction approximation path from e/φ(n) to d.

analogies.analogy: Think of n = p × q like mixing two colors to get a new color. Given the final color, it's extremely hard to reverse-engineer the original two colors, especially if they're chosen from millions of options.

Raw RSA is like a locked box where you can rearrange contents. Proper padding is like adding random junk and a unique seal - any tampering is detected, and you can't predict what happens when you modify contents.
The public key is like a mailbox slot - anyone can drop messages in (encrypt), but only the mailbox owner with the private key (unique key) can open and read messages (decrypt).

Without padding, RSA is like having a transparent envelope - if you know what's in two envelopes and their product, you can figure out the contents. Padding adds opaque wrapping that breaks this property.
